rationale: A0 shows PowerShell launched with '-WindowStyle hidden' executing a DownloadFile call to a bit.ly-shortened URL ('http://bit.ly/L3g1tCrad1e') — a classic obfuscated cradle pattern used to stage payloads while evading casual inspection. EV-003 confirms the download succeeded: the target file 'Default_File_Path.ps1' was written to the Administrator's home directory by the same PowerShell process (PID 1668). EV-001 establishes the parent chain: an interactive cmd.exe spawned from Explorer under the high-integrity corp\Administrator context, indicating either direct console access or a prior interactive session — not a scheduled task or remote management baseline. EV-002 is a routine PSScriptPolicyTest temp file created during PowerShell initialization and is not independently malicious, but confirms the same process. The combination of hidden-window flag, URL shortener obfuscation, confirmed file drop of a .ps1 payload, and high-integrity execution context constitutes confirmed malicious staging activity; the content and subsequent execution of the dropped script are not yet evidenced but the download itself is confirmed.
